text: Trochars are placed in the abdominal wall for laparoscopic access. The surgeon performs a robotic-assisted Heller myotomy and Thor funduplication, with intraoperative endoscopy confirming asthenosis at the gastroesophageal junction due to achalasia.

The surgeon begins by dissecting the hiatus, noting lax adhesions between the liver, stomach, and previous wrap due to a hiatal hernia with the Nissen partially slipped into the chest. Gentle circumferential dissection releases adhesions among the stomach, diaphragm, and caudate lobe of the liver. Short gastric vessels are divided, and a retrogastric window is created to pass a Penrose drain for easier dissection. The mediastinum is dissected until the esophagus is identified, followed by circumferential dissection of the esophagus extending fifteen centimeters into the chest. The Nissen funduplication is then repositioned into the abdomen along with the GE junction, and the funduplication is taken down.

An intraoperative endoscopy reveals a dilated esophagus and a tight gastroesophageal junction. A 48 French tapered bougie is introduced to guide the myotomy. The surgeon performs a myotomy on the esophagus using electrocautery and monopolar scissors, transecting both longitudinal and circular muscle layers for five centimeters into the chest.

The surgeon transects the transverse oblique and circular muscle layers at the gastroesophageal junction and gastric portion of the myotomy, measuring approximately three centimeters. The Bauhaus nerve is preserved during the procedure. An intraoperative endoscopy is performed, showing no evidence of leaks, and the GE junction is traversed smoothly.

The surgeon approximates the pillars of the hiatus with interrupted stitches to restore anatomical alignment. Several stitches are placed between the left and right grooves of the myotomy to keep it open. The stomach is then flipped over the myotomy using the Dor technique. Hemostasis is checked to ensure no bleeding.
A postoperative upper GI contrast study reveals the resolution of esophageal dilation, with the contrast passing smoothly through the GI junction and no visible leaks.
